title: 2025/10/16 - TI (2 of 3)
displayTitle: 2025-10-16 - Transport and Infrastructure Committee - Regulatory Systems (Transport) Amendment Bill
description: 16 October 2025 | Transport and Infrastructure Committee Regulatory Systems (Transport) Amendment Bill The New Zealand Automobile Association Incorporated Dylan Thomsen, Communications, Research & Road Safety Manager Terry Collins, Principal Policy Advisor Maritime Union of New Zealand Paul Baggott, Vice President of Wellington Branch Rob Egan, Advisor Navigation Safety Group - Regional Harbourmasters Grant Nalder, Wellington Regional Harbourmaster Runze Liu
descriptionSummary: Regulatory Systems (Transport) Amendment Bill. The New Zealand Automobile Association Incorporated. Dylan Thomsen, Communications, Research & Road Safety Manager

chunks.text: So it is our normal process, which you are well aware of. We, um, give you, give you 10 minutes. I'll give you a call at about four minutes to go if you're still talking. Um, and, but we do like to have some, uh, questions. So, um, we'll get straight into it and, um, the time, as I say, starts now. Right. Uh, Kiata, thank you for the opportunity to speak.

I'm Dylan Thompson and my colleague Terry Collins is here beside me from the Automobile Association. Uh, we are part of the AA transport policy and advocacy team. And, um, our work involves using surveys of AA members, academic research, and our 18 district councils around the country are volunteers to, uh, help us form positions and, and call for changes in transport that we think would make it better or safer.

Uh, we, we agree with this bill, the goal of better and modernized regulation. Um, essentially we are here to support it and also highlight some areas we think the select committee should focus on in your dealings with officials, um, that are gonna be needed to make sure that it delivers the best results.

Uh, there's three particular areas we are focused on. First I'm gonna talk about is digital driver's licenses. So I can remember the first driver's license I got was just a piece of paper, and then in the nineties moved on to a plastic Bill. Doesn't look old enough that way. That's true. Uh, and then in the nineties we moved on to plastic cards with photos.

And we see this change towards a digital option as just being another step into the modern world. We've been really pleased to see comments from the government about the fact that this is going to be an option and people who don't want to have a digital license are still gonna have the option to retain a physical license. We think that's really important.

The key thing for us will be the challenge of making sure the system gets the balance right between being very secure and strong in that regard, but also quite easy and pain-free to use for drivers as they go about their daily life. And so that is one of the challenges we see for officials in developing this.

For it to work well, it's gonna have to get that balance right. And our advice would be that, um, you know, systems like this are in use in many places overseas. Most Australian jurisdiction states have their own version. We would hope that officials are going to look over there, identify the ones that they think work best, and use that as a model for New Zealand to follow rather than us looking to reinvent the wheel.

Similar to digital licenses, uh, we support the move towards allowing digital versions of warranty, fitness and vehicle licensing, registration labels, um, replacing the, you know, physical stickers and labels on the windscreens. One of the key risks we would like the select committee to have in their mind is wanting to make sure that as these changes happen, we don't have a situation where people feel that they are going to be more able to get away with having an unwarranted or unregistered vehicle without those physical labels.

So we think that that's gonna be really important, focusing on still being able to have very quick and easy roadside enforcement because it's very important for us that people are still going to be getting warrant vehicles checked road legal with a warrant and contributing through their registration fee.

And the other aspect of that is also the system is gonna need to do a really good job for the people that are using the digital options of providing reminders, alerts, some sort of notification so that people know when their next inspection is due and coming up and people know when they next need to make those payments.

Finally, the aspects of the bill dealing with electronic communication. So, um, people do the majority of their communication online these days. So once again, we, we supporting the moves to enable fines, infringement, notices, official information to be communicated through electronic means rather than the post.

Uh, we've wanted to see a move like this for some time, um, particularly around penalties because at the moment we have situation where if somebody is driving a vehicle past a speed camera, for example, right now, and they're breaking the limit, it may well be weeks until they get a ticket through the post. Um, the evidence on behavior change and sort of crime and punishment theory is the quicker you have consequences from an action, the better in terms of changing behavior.

So we are right behind the move towards electronic communication, just like the others. It's in the detail of how we do it, making sure it's done well. Um, so two things that we just want to sort of put in the select committee's mind. One is New Zealand, like all countries, is moving towards an increased amount of automated enforcement, which goes back to the, the previous point about vehicle registration.

This is going to be more and more important that vehicles are able to be identified and also have an accurate UpToDate registration and a, and a registered owner for some of that automated enforcement to work. And the second thing is that the communication system for people receiving these things via email or text, things like that, is going to need to be really carefully designed because there are some fish hooks, some genuine fish hooks where people's span filters or different, different aspects of their email system means

that something legitimate could never land in their primary inbox and they may genuinely not see it. It. So our idea is to help mitigate those risks. Uh, we think the system needs to have some good safeguards to try and confirm delivery and receipt of messages, uh, that we use multiple channels.

So the options of email, text, online portals gonna be really important for very clear and easy to understand information for drivers and vehicle owners out there to know how they can check their contact details, update their contact details, and look for any notices, tickets, and like that.

And like all the others, we're still gonna have to keep that fallback option of the physical post available. Uh, so in summary, we support the Bill's intent to modernize New Zealand's regulatory system. We see a lot of positives in it. Key thing will just be making sure that we do that in a well-designed way to avoid any unintended negative consequences from these digital changes.

Um, thank you for the opportunity to speak. I'm Paul Bagot, the Seafarer and Vice President of the Wellington, branch of the Maritime New Zealand, um, union of New Zealand.

Our union represents both dock workers and seafarers working on New Zealand coastal vessels. I'm joined today by Rob Egan, who advises MUS on government and legislative issues. M has two issues with this bill. First, the protections for seafarers in the event of something goes wrong during their employment.

If a seafarer has to be left behind due to illness or other reasons, the owner should be obliged to return them to their home. If a vessel is lost, the seafarers of employment ends immediately, their employer should continue, their employment should continue until there has been a return to their home or another degreed destination.

Unfortunately, these circumstances are rare, but they are serious and even life-threatening situations for seafarers, and they deserve to have proper employment protections when they occur. The second issue affects seafarers in the New Zealand economy. Every day, section 1 9 8 of the Maritime Transport Act creates the so-called open coast crisis, that open coast policy.

This allowed international vessels visiting New Zealand to carry domestic cargoes and foreign vessels to permanently work new Zealand's coastal workers with a permit from the minister. While ignoring New Zealand's wage and labor conditions laws, this policy nearly wiped out New Zealand's coastal shipping fleet and cost hundreds of Kiwi jobs that was back in the nineties.

It undermines New Zealand sovereignty and resilience, leaves us dependent on foreign shippers to carry vital goods to the regions and pick up exports for foreign markets. It turns illegal blind eye to the exploitation of OCC Fairers in New Zealand's waters. Right now, wholesome cement is moving to replace New Zealand, flagged New Zealand crew, coastal carrier, the MV buffalo with foreign flag accrued vessels.

This would cost 32 New Zealand jobs and see foreign seafarers brought in to work for unclear wages. This bill is an opportunity to fix the broken section of 1 9 8 of the Maline Transport Act and end the failed open post policy. It is time to ensure New Zealand cargo is carried by workers employed fairly under New Zealand wages and conditions.
